The crude birth rate measured in people per thousand

variables.name: births
variables.comment: The total number of births in the world (B#30).
variables.equation: IF( TIME >= population_equilibrium_time , deaths , ( total_fertility * Population_15_To_44 * 0.5 / reproductive_lifetime ) )

variables.name: completed multiplier from perceived lifetime
variables.comment:
COMPENSATORY MULTIPLIER FROM PERCEIVED LIFE
		         EXPECTANCY (CMPLE#36).

variables.name: desired completed family size
variables.equation: IF( TIME >= zero_population_growth_time_s , 2, desired_completed_family_size_normal * family_response_to_social_norm * social_family_size_normal )

variables.name: fraction services allocated to fertility control
variables.comment:
FRACTION OF SERVICES ALLOCATED TO FERTILITY CONTROL
		         (FSAFC#48).

variables.name: maximum total fertility normal
variables.comment:
The normal maximum fertility that would be realized
		        if people had sufficient food and perfect health.
		         (MTFN#33)

variables.name: reproductive lifetime
variables.comment: The number of years people can reproduce (RLT#30.1)

variables.name: population equilibrium time
variables.comment:
The time at which, as a model test, the population
		         is forced to remain constant. (PET#30.2)

variables.name: total fertility
variables.equation: MIN ( maximum_total_fertility , ( maximum_total_fertility * ( 1 - fertility_control_effectiveness ) + desired_total_fertility * fertility_control_effectiveness ) )

variables.name: deaths
variables.comment:
The total number of deaths per year for all age
		         groups (D#17).

lookupTables.name: family response to social norm table
lookupTables.comment:
The table relating income expectations to family
		         size (FRSNT#41.1).

lookupTables.name: fecundity multiplier table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating life expectancy to fecundity
		         (FMT#34.1).

lookupTables.name: fertility control effectiveness table
lookupTables.comment: Fertility control effectiveness table (FCET#45.2).

lookupTables.name: fraction services allocated to fertility control table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating the need for fertility control to
		         services allocated. (FSAFCT#48.1).

lookupTables.name: social family size normal table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating material well being to family size
		         (SFNT#39.1)

lookupTables.name: crowding multiplier from industry table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating industrial output to crowding
		         (CMIT#27.1).

lookupTables.name: fraction of population urban table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating population to the fraction of
		         population that is urban (FPUT#26.1).

lookupTables.name: health services per capita table
lookupTables.comment:
The table relating service output to health services
		         (HSAPCT#21.1).

lookupTables.name: lifetime multiplier from food table
lookupTables.comment:
The table ralating relative food to the life
		         expectancy multiplier for food (LMFT#20.1)

lookupTables.name: lifetime multiplier from health services 1 table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating effective health care to life
		         expectancy (LMHS1T#24.1).

lookupTables.name: lifetime multiplier from health services 2 table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating effective health care to life
		         expectancy (LMHS2T#25.1)

lookupTables.name: lifetime multiplier from persistent pollution table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating persistent pollution to life
		         expectancy (LMPT#29.1)

lookupTables.name: fraction of capital allocated to obtaining resources 1 table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating the fraction of resources remaining
		        to capital allocated to resource extraction
		         (FCAOR1T#135.1).

lookupTables.name: fraction of capital allocated to obtaining resources 2 table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating the fraction of resources remaining to capital allocated to resource  extraction
		 (FCAOR2T#136.1). Note that the original LTG model uses (0,1), (0.1,0.2),  (0.2,0.1), as the first entries but this table is changed to replicate  scenarios in the new book.

lookupTables.name: per capita resource use mult table
lookupTables.comment:
Table relating industrial output to resource usage
		         per capita (PCRUMT#132.1).

comments.name: Comment 19
comments.text: Use the scenario slider to select the scenario to run.

comments.name: Comment 20
comments.text: Scenarios 1 to 10 are the ones described in the third edition of the Limits to Growth book.

comments.name: Comment 21
comments.text: The use of scenario 0 is described in the Scenario Control Detail page.

comments.name: Comment 22
comments.text: The scenarios are described on the Scenario Descriptions page.

comments.name: Comment 36
comments.text: Note var means variable and var< means variable but less than the value above.

comments.name: Comment 39
comments.text: Scenario 1 — Described on page 168 of third edition of Limits to Growth. This shows "tie behavior of |<<NnN>>|World3 when it is run as is, wit) numbers we consider a realistic description of the situation as it appeared on average during the latter part of the twentieth century. In 1972 we called it tie standard run." |<<NnN>>|In all the remaining scenarios the changes start in 2002, except for scenario 10, which starts in 1982. |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 2 - Described on page 171. Scenario 1 plus optimistic assumptions about the system . Initial |<<NnN>>|nonrenewable resources are doubled and the fraction of capital allocated to obtaining resources is |<<NnN>>|increased substantially to about 5% of industrial Page 210 states that "scenario 2 will be basis |<<NnN>>|of comparison for the technology and policy changes that follow." In other words, scenario 2 is tie |<<NnN>>|business as usual scenario. It is the problem to solve. |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 3 — Page 210. Scenario 2 plus more accessible nonrenewable resources and pollution control|<<NnN>>|technology, all starting in 2002. "This allows much higher welfare for more people after 2040 because of |<<NnN>>|fewer negative effects from pollution. But food production does ultimately decline, drawing capital from |<<NnN>>|the industrial sector and triggering a collapse." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 4 - Page 214. Scenario 3 plus higher land yield, via an increase in Land Yield Technology. "The |<<NnN>>|world's farmers end up trying to squeeze more and more food output from less and less land. This proves unsustainable." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 5 — Page 216. Scenario 4 plus longer agricultural land life. "The result is a postponement of the collapse at the end of the twenty-first century." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|

comments.name: Comment 40
comments.text: Scenario 6 — Page 218. Scenario 5 plus more efficient use of nonrenewable resources, via improvement |<<NnN>>|in Resource Conservation Technology. "Now the world is developing powerful technologies for pollution |<<NnN>>|abatement, land yield enhancement, land protection, and conservation of nonrenewable resources all at |<<NnN>>|once. All these technologies are assumed to involve costs and to tae 20 years to be fully implemented. In |<<NnN>>|combination they permit a large and prosperous simulated world, until the bliss starts declining in |<<NnN>>|response to the accumulated cost of the technologies." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 7 — Page 240. Scenario 2 (not 5) plus a family size of 2 and 100% effective fertility control |<<NnN>>|effectiveness. "Because of age structure momentum, tie population continues to grow for another |<<NnN>>|generation. But the slower population growth permit industrial output to rise faster, until it is stopped by the cost of dealing with rising pollution, as in scenario 2." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 8 - Page 242. Scenario 7 plus "a definition of enough. This world has decided to aim for an |<<NnN>>|industrial output per capita level for everyone that is about 10% higher than the world average in the year 2000." The result is society "an extend somewhat the 'golden period' of fairly high human welfare |<<NnN>>|between 2020 and 2040 in scenario 7. But pollution increasingly stresses agricultural resources. Per capita food production deadlines, eventually bringing down life expectancy and population." |<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 9 — Page 244. Scenario 8 plus the technology improvements introduced in scenarios 3, 4, 5, and 6. "The resulting society is sustainable: Nearly 8 billion people live with high human welfare and a |<<NnN>>|continuously declining ecological footprint."|<<NnN>>||<<NnN>>|Scenario 10 — Page 248. Same as scenario 9 except the changes are introduced 20 years earlier, in |<<NnN>>|1982. "The scenario 10 population is able to maintain its standard of living and support its improving |<<NnN>>|technologies with no problems. This society,' has a more pleasant environment, more resources, more |<<NnN>>|degrees of freedom; it is farther from it limits, less on the edge than the society in scenario 9. But the |<<NnN>>|world society of 1982 did not grasp the opportunity."

comments.name: Comment 53
comments.text: All this structure is just a way to allows changes to the scenario number to be used to replicate each scenario. When the scenario number is 0 (or ... use custom is 1)  the ...s values used match exactly the input constant (shown in magenta).
